# Heartwood — Full API Reference

> Nostr private keys are pasted into web apps, stored in browser extensions, and copied to the clipboard. Heartwood keeps them on dedicated hardware instead.

Heartwood is open-source Nostr signing built on nsec-tree. `heartwood-bridge` is a headless, keyless daemon that connects Nostr relays to a USB-tethered hardware signer -- an ESP32 running the [heartwood-esp32](https://github.com/forgesworn/heartwood-esp32) firmware. The bridge holds no key material and never sees plaintext: NIP-44 decryption, NIP-46 request dispatch, per-client permissions, session/replay protection, and signing all happen on the device itself, inline over USB. The bridge is only the device's relay connection, and nothing more.

Repository: https://github.com/forgesworn/heartwood
Licence: MIT

For a quick overview, see [llms.txt](./llms.txt).

---

## Table of Contents

1. [Architecture](#architecture)
2. [heartwood-core API](#heartwood-core-api)
3. [NIP-46 Client Integration](#nip-46-client-integration)
4. [Security Model](#security-model)
5. [Deployment](#deployment)

---

## Architecture

Heartwood is a Rust workspace with three crates and one runtime binary, `heartwood-bridge`:

```
heartwood-bridge      The product -- headless, keyless relay-to-USB signing daemon
  ├── main.rs         Entry point: --version/--help, config load, task wiring
  ├── config.rs       Env / config.json / bridge.secret resolution (HEARTWOOD_DATA_DIR)
  ├── serial.rs       Blocking serial session: auth, master discovery, 0x10 -> 0x35 request/response
  ├── bridge.rs       Serial worker thread: sequential job processing, response broadcast
  ├── relay.rs        Per-relay websocket task: subscribe, de-dup, forward, publish, reconnect
  ├── dedup.rs        Bounded de-duplication set for cross-relay request ids
  ├── event.rs        NIP-46 request events and the 0x10 (ENCRYPTED_REQUEST) frame payload
  └── npub.rs         NIP-19 npub decoding (for building relay subscription filters)

heartwood-frame       Shared binary serial frame codec (no I/O)
  └── lib.rs          magic "HW" / type / big-endian length / IEEE CRC-32; frame type constants

heartwood-core        nsec-tree derivation primitive (crate name `nsec-tree-rs`), no I/O
  ├── root.rs         Master secret creation (mnemonic, nsec, nsec-bytes)
  ├── derive.rs       HMAC-SHA256 child key derivation
  ├── persona.rs      Named persona derivation
  ├── proof.rs        Linkage proofs (blind and full)
  ├── recover.rs      Key scanning and recovery
  ├── encoding.rs     bech32 npub/nsec encoding
  ├── validate.rs     Purpose string validation
  └── types.rs        TreeRoot, Identity, Persona, LinkageProof, HeartwoodError
```

Data flow: NIP-46 clients → Nostr relays → `heartwood-bridge` (subscribes, de-duplicates) → USB serial → the hardware signer. The signer does all of the cryptography inline -- NIP-44 decryption, NIP-46 method dispatch, permission/policy checks, re-encryption, and signing -- and hands back a signed event, which the bridge republishes verbatim to every relay. The bridge never decrypts a request, never constructs an event, and never touches key material.

`heartwood-core` is no longer a dependency of `heartwood-bridge` or of any binary in this repo. It is kept as a standalone reference/compatibility library with frozen cross-implementation test vectors, while the same HMAC-SHA256 derivation logic now runs on-device, in the `heartwood-esp32` firmware.

---

## heartwood-core API

The crate's package name is `nsec-tree-rs` (imported as `nsec_tree_rs`); the workspace directory is still `crates/heartwood-core`.

### Types

```rust
/// Master tree root. Owns the secret; zeroes on drop.
pub struct TreeRoot {
    secret: Zeroizing<[u8; 32]>,     // private, not accessible outside the crate
    pub master_pubkey: String,        // npub bech32
}

impl TreeRoot {
    pub fn destroy(mut self);         // Explicitly zero the secret
}

/// A derived child identity.
pub struct Identity {
    pub npub: String,                 // npub bech32
    pub private_key: Zeroizing<[u8; 32]>,
    pub public_key: [u8; 32],
    pub purpose: String,
    pub index: u32,
}

impl Identity {
    pub fn nsec(&self) -> String;     // Compute bech32 nsec on demand (never stored)
    pub fn zeroize(&mut self);        // Zero the private key bytes
}

/// A named persona wrapping an identity.
pub struct Persona {
    pub identity: Identity,
    pub name: String,
    pub index: u32,
}

/// A linkage proof (blind or full).
pub struct LinkageProof {
    pub master_pubkey: String,        // 64-char lowercase hex
    pub child_pubkey: String,         // 64-char lowercase hex
    pub purpose: Option<String>,      // None for blind proofs
    pub index: Option<u32>,           // None for blind proofs
    pub attestation: String,          // "nsec-tree:own|{master_hex}|{child_hex}" (blind) or
                                       // "nsec-tree:link|{master_hex}|{child_hex}|{purpose}|{index}" (full)
    pub signature: String,            // 128-char lowercase hex (BIP-340 Schnorr)
}

/// Error type for all heartwood-core operations.
pub enum HeartwoodError {
    IndexOverflow,
    InvalidPurpose(String),
    InvalidMnemonic,
    InvalidNsec,
    InvalidNpub,
    InvalidProof,
    InvalidScanRange,
    InvalidPersonaName(String),
    Derivation(String),
}
```

### Constants

```rust
pub const MAX_INDEX: u32 = 0xFFFF_FFFF;
pub const DEFAULT_SCAN_RANGE: u32 = 20;
pub const MAX_SCAN_RANGE: u32 = 10_000;
pub const DOMAIN_PREFIX: &[u8] = b"nsec-tree\0";
pub const NSEC_ROOT_LABEL: &[u8] = b"nsec-tree-root";
pub const MNEMONIC_PATH: &str = "m/44'/1237'/727'/0'/0'";
```

### Root Creation

```rust
/// Create a TreeRoot from a BIP-39 mnemonic phrase.
///
/// 1. Validate mnemonic → 2. PBKDF2 seed (optional passphrase)
/// 3. BIP-32 derive at m/44'/1237'/727'/0'/0'
/// 4. Use private key directly as tree root secret (NO HMAC intermediate)
pub fn from_mnemonic(mnemonic: &str, passphrase: Option<&str>) -> Result<TreeRoot, HeartwoodError>;

/// Create a TreeRoot from a bech32 nsec string via HMAC intermediate.
///
/// tree_root_secret = HMAC-SHA256(key=nsec_bytes, msg="nsec-tree-root")
/// IMPORTANT: This produces a DIFFERENT root than from_mnemonic. They are not interchangeable.
pub fn from_nsec(nsec: &str) -> Result<TreeRoot, HeartwoodError>;

/// Create a TreeRoot from raw 32-byte nsec via HMAC intermediate.
/// Same as from_nsec but accepts raw bytes instead of bech32.
pub fn from_nsec_bytes(nsec_bytes: &[u8; 32]) -> Result<TreeRoot, HeartwoodError>;

/// Validate an nsec and return its npub (no tree derivation, no HMAC).
/// Used in bunker mode where the nsec signs directly.
pub fn npub_from_nsec(nsec: &str) -> Result<String, HeartwoodError>;
```

### Child Key Derivation

```rust
/// Derive a child identity from a TreeRoot.
///
/// context = b"nsec-tree\0" || purpose_utf8 || 0x00 || index_u32_big_endian
/// child_secret = HMAC-SHA256(key=root_secret, msg=context)
///
/// If the derived scalar exceeds the secp256k1 curve order, the index is
/// incremented until a valid key is found (extremely rare).
pub fn derive(root: &TreeRoot, purpose: &str, index: u32) -> Result<Identity, HeartwoodError>;

/// Derive a child identity from an existing identity (arbitrary depth).
///
/// Creates a transient TreeRoot from the identity's private key using
/// create_tree_root directly (no HMAC intermediate), then derives a child
/// from it. Note there is no `root` parameter -- the identity's own private
/// key is the parent for this derivation.
pub fn derive_from_identity(
    identity: &Identity,
    purpose: &str,
    index: u32,
) -> Result<Identity, HeartwoodError>;
```

### Persona Derivation

```rust
/// Derive a named persona: purpose = "nostr:persona:{name}".
///
/// If index is None, defaults to 0.
pub fn derive_persona(
    root: &TreeRoot,
    name: &str,
    index: Option<u32>,
) -> Result<Persona, HeartwoodError>;

/// Derive a sub-identity from an existing persona.
///
/// Note there is no `root` parameter -- the persona's own identity is the
/// parent for this derivation.
pub fn derive_from_persona(
    persona: &Persona,
    purpose: &str,
    index: Option<u32>,
) -> Result<Identity, HeartwoodError>;
```

### Linkage Proofs

```rust
/// Create a blind proof (does not reveal purpose or index).
///
/// attestation = "nsec-tree:own|{master_pubkey_hex}|{child_pubkey_hex}"
/// signature = BIP-340 Schnorr sign(master_secret, attestation)
pub fn create_blind_proof(
    root: &TreeRoot,
    child: &Identity,
) -> Result<LinkageProof, HeartwoodError>;

/// Create a full proof (reveals purpose and index).
///
/// attestation = "nsec-tree:link|{master_pubkey_hex}|{child_pubkey_hex}|{purpose}|{index}"
/// signature = BIP-340 Schnorr sign(master_secret, attestation)
pub fn create_full_proof(
    root: &TreeRoot,
    child: &Identity,
) -> Result<LinkageProof, HeartwoodError>;

/// Verify a linkage proof.
///
/// Reconstructs the canonical attestation string from the proof's own
/// fields and checks the BIP-340 Schnorr signature against it (not against
/// the caller-supplied `attestation` string directly, though the two must
/// match). Returns Ok(false) -- not an error -- for a structurally invalid
/// or non-matching proof; Err(HeartwoodError::InvalidProof) only for
/// unparseable hex in the pubkey/signature fields.
pub fn verify_proof(proof: &LinkageProof) -> Result<bool, HeartwoodError>;
```

### Recovery

```rust
/// Scan derived keys across multiple purposes to find existing identities.
///
/// For each purpose, derives keys at indices 0..scan_range and returns any
/// that produce valid secp256k1 keys. Default scan range: 20. Max: 10,000.
pub fn recover(
    root: &TreeRoot,
    purposes: &[String],
    scan_range: Option<u32>,
) -> Result<std::collections::HashMap<String, Vec<Identity>>, HeartwoodError>;
```

### Encoding

```rust
pub fn encode_npub(pubkey: &[u8; 32]) -> String;   // → "npub1..."
pub fn encode_nsec(seckey: &[u8; 32]) -> String;   // → "nsec1..."
pub fn decode_npub(npub: &str) -> Result<[u8; 32], HeartwoodError>;
pub fn decode_nsec(nsec: &str) -> Result<Zeroizing<[u8; 32]>, HeartwoodError>;
```

### Purpose Validation

```rust
/// Validate a purpose string. Rules:
/// - Non-empty, max 255 bytes
/// - No null bytes (0x00) or pipe characters (|)
/// - Not whitespace-only
pub fn validate_purpose(purpose: &str) -> Result<(), HeartwoodError>;
```

---

## NIP-46 Client Integration

Heartwood does not define its own wire protocol on the client side -- any standard NIP-46 (Nostr Connect) client works. The connection string is a `bunker://` or `nostrconnect://` URI:

```
bunker://{signer-pubkey}?relay=wss://{relay}&secret={secret}
nostrconnect://{signer-pubkey}?relay=wss://{relay}&secret={secret}&perms={kinds}
```

`heartwood-bridge` never parses or dispatches a NIP-46 method itself. It forwards the request's NIP-44 ciphertext to the hardware signer as a single `0x10` (`ENCRYPTED_REQUEST`) frame and republishes whatever the signer returns as a `0x35` (`SIGN_ENVELOPE_RESPONSE`) frame, byte-for-byte. All of the following happen on the device, in the `heartwood-esp32` firmware -- not in this repo:

- Standard NIP-46 method dispatch (`get_public_key`, `sign_event`, NIP-44 encrypt/decrypt)
- Client approval, per-client kind restrictions, and rate limiting
- Session handling and replay protection

There is no Tor hidden service and no web UI serving a pairing QR code. Pairing -- generating the `bunker://` connection string, approving new clients, and setting per-client permissions -- is configured on the device itself, normally via [Sapwood](https://sapwood.forgesworn.dev) (Web Serial/USB), or offline with the `provision` CLI. See [`heartwood-esp32`](https://github.com/forgesworn/heartwood-esp32) for the firmware-side protocol implementation.

NIP-42 AUTH is unsupported: the bridge holds no key, so it cannot sign an auth challenge. Relays that require AUTH for kind:24133 requests are out of scope.

---

## Security Model

| Leaves the bridge | Never touches the bridge |
|--------------------|---------------------------|
| Ciphertext (forwarded verbatim) | Plaintext requests/responses |
| Signed events (republished verbatim) | Master or derived private keys |
| Request timing/metadata (visible to relays regardless) | Encryption/decryption keys |

### heartwood-bridge

- **No key, no plaintext on the host.** All NIP-44 decryption, NIP-46 dispatch, and signing happen on the USB-tethered hardware signer; the bridge only pumps ciphertext in and signed events out.
- **`bridge.secret`** authenticates the serial session (constant-time compared in firmware; setting it on the device requires a physical button hold). Treat the file as a local secret (mode 0600).
- **No inbound listener.** The bridge only makes outbound WebSocket connections to relays -- no open ports, no Tor hidden service (Tor previously existed solely to front the now-retired web UI, which no longer exists in this repo).
- **NIP-42 AUTH is unsupported.** The bridge holds no key, so it cannot sign an auth challenge; relays that require AUTH for kind:24133 are out of scope.
- **Cross-relay de-duplication only, no policy.** The bridge de-duplicates the same request id arriving from multiple relays so it isn't forwarded twice; it has no visibility into approval, kind restrictions, or rate limiting beyond a NACK (`0x15`) if the device rejects a request.

### heartwood-core

- Keys derived via HMAC-SHA256 (RFC 2104) -- not BIP-32 extended keys at the child level
- Master secret held in `zeroize::Zeroizing<[u8; 32]>`, zeroed from RAM on drop; intermediate secrets are explicitly zeroised
- `TreeRoot`'s secret field is private (`pub(crate)` accessor only) -- the raw secret cannot escape `heartwood-core` except through `Identity`, which the caller owns
- Kept in the workspace as a standalone reference/compatibility library, cross-validated against the TypeScript reference implementation with frozen test vectors (59 tests total, including 5 frozen vectors); no longer a runtime dependency of `heartwood-bridge` or any binary in this repo

---

## Deployment

### Quick Start

```bash
curl -sL https://github.com/forgesworn/heartwood/releases/latest/download/install.sh | sudo bash
```

This detects the host architecture (aarch64, x86_64, armv7, or riscv64), downloads the matching release binary, verifies its SHA-256 checksum, creates a `heartwood` system user in the `dialout` group (for USB serial access), creates `/var/lib/heartwood`, and installs -- enabled but not started -- a `heartwood-bridge` systemd service.

Or build from source:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/forgesworn/heartwood
cd heartwood
cargo build --release -p heartwood-bridge
```

Cross-compile for another architecture:

```bash
cargo install cross
cross build --release --target aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu -p heartwood-bridge
```

Docker (multi-arch: aarch64/armv7/amd64):

```bash
docker pull ghcr.io/forgesworn/heartwood:<version>
```

### Provisioning

The hardware signer's keys, and the `bridge.secret` the bridge needs to authenticate its USB session, are provisioned onto the device over USB -- not by this repo:

- **[Sapwood](https://sapwood.forgesworn.dev)** (recommended) -- a browser-based (Web Serial/USB) provisioning wizard for most users.
- **`provision` CLI** (in [`heartwood-esp32`](https://github.com/forgesworn/heartwood-esp32)) -- the offline, air-gapped alternative, for the highest-security no-browser tier.

Either path writes the master identity into the signer's own storage and writes a `bridge.secret` file that must be copied into the bridge's data directory before first start.

### Environment Variables

| Variable | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|--------------|
| `HEARTWOOD_DATA_DIR` | `/var/lib/heartwood` | Data directory: `config.json` and `bridge.secret` live here |
| `HEARTWOOD_SERIAL_PORT` | (none) | Required -- serial device to talk to, e.g. `/dev/ttyUSB0`. Falls back to `config.json`'s `serial_port` field |
| `HEARTWOOD_RELAYS` | built-in default list | Comma-separated relay URLs. Falls back to `config.json`'s `relays` array |
| `RUST_LOG` | (none) | Tracing filter (e.g. `info`, `heartwood_bridge=debug`) |

`config.json` (`{ "serial_port": "...", "relays": [...] }`) is a plain file written by whoever sets the box up -- there is no privileged process that owns it. The binary itself takes no flags beyond `--version`/`-V` and `--help`/`-h`.

### Systemd

Installed by the release install script as `heartwood-bridge.service`:

```bash
sudo systemctl start heartwood-bridge
sudo systemctl status heartwood-bridge
sudo journalctl -u heartwood-bridge -f
```

### File Paths

| Path | Contents |
|------|----------|
| `/var/lib/heartwood/config.json` | Serial port and relay configuration |
| `/var/lib/heartwood/bridge.secret` | USB session secret provisioned by Sapwood or the `provision` CLI (mode 0600) |

There is no encrypted secret file, no bunker keypair, and no audit log on the bridge host -- the bridge holds nothing sensitive beyond `bridge.secret`, which authenticates the USB session rather than signing anything.

---

## Ecosystem

- [nsec-tree](https://github.com/forgesworn/nsec-tree) — TypeScript reference implementation (169 tests, frozen vectors)
- [shamir-words](https://github.com/forgesworn/shamir-words) — Mnemonic threshold backup splitting
- [canary-kit](https://github.com/forgesworn/canary-kit) — Dead man's switch (pairs with Heartwood duress persona)
- [bark](https://github.com/forgesworn/bark) — Browser extension for NIP-07 signing via Heartwood
- [bray](https://github.com/forgesworn/bray) — Nostr MCP server (AI agents sign through Heartwood)
- [toll-booth](https://github.com/forgesworn/toll-booth) — L402 API payments (per-service signing identities)
